title: EXPOSÉ VIII CLASS GROUPS OF ABELIAN AND TRIANGULATED CATEGORIES. PERFECT COMPLEXES.

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VIII CLASS GROUPS OF ABELIAN AND TRIANGULATED CATEGORIES. PERFECT COMPLEXES.

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VIII
CLASS GROUPS OF ABELIAN
AND TRIANGULATED CATEGORIES. PERFECT COMPLEXES.by A. Grothendieck, written up by I. Bucur

In the present exposé, we rapidly sketch the notions and results which will be indispensable to us in the following exposés, concerning in particular pseudo-coherent and perfect complexes. A more complete account of the latter, with more detailed proofs, will appear in the first exposés of the seminar on the Riemann-Roch theorem for schemes, which will follow the present seminar.(*)
1. The case of abelian categories.
Let \(C\) be an abelian category and let \(D\) be a full subcategory of \(C\). We say that a function \(f\) from \(D\) into an abelian group \(G\) is additive if, for every exact sequence of \(C\)
\[0\longrightarrow E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow0,\]
such that \(E'\), \(E''\), \(E\) are objects of \(D\), we have
Let \(f\) be an additive function from \(D\) into an abelian group \(G\). The following properties are easy consequences of the definition:
β) \(f(X)=f(Y)\) if \(X\) and \(Y\) are isomorphic.
γ) \(f(X\oplus Y)=f(X)+f(Y)\).
δ) Let \(E=(E^i,d^i)\) be a finite complex of \(D\) such that the objects \(B^{i+1}=\operatorname{Im}(d^i)\), \(Z^i=\operatorname{Ker}(d^i)\) and \(H^i(E)=Z^i/B^i\) are in \(D\). Then we have
ε) If \(F\in\operatorname{Ob}D\) has a finite filtration,
\[0=F_0\subset F_1\subset\cdots\subset F_n=F,\]
and if we assume that for \(0<i\leq n\), \(F_i\) and \(F_i/F_{i-1}\) are objects of \(D\), then
φ) If \(D\) is stable under infinite direct sums, then \(f=0\).
To prove δ) we use the exact sequences:
\[0\longrightarrow Z^i\longrightarrow E^i\longrightarrow B^{i+1}\longrightarrow0\] \[0\longrightarrow B^i\longrightarrow Z^i\longrightarrow H^i(E)\longrightarrow0.\]
The proof of ε) is done by induction on \(n\), using the exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow F_{n-1}\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F/F_{n-1}\longrightarrow0\).
To prove φ), let \(E\in\operatorname{Ob}D\) and \(F=\bigoplus_{n\in\mathbb Z^+}E_n\), \(E_n=E\), \(n\in\mathbb Z^+\). Then \(E\oplus F\simeq F\) and, applying γ), we have
\[f(F)=f(F)+f(E)\quad\text{hence}\quad f(E)=0.\]

If we consider, for every abelian group \(G\), the abelian group \(A_D(G)\) of additive functions from \(D\) into \(G\), we obtain in an obvious way a covariant functor \(A_D:\mathbf{Ab}\longrightarrow\mathbf{Ab}\). The functor \(A_D\) is representable. A representing pair \((K_D(C),\varphi)\) can be obtained in the following way:
Denoting by \(L\) the free abelian group generated by the objects of \(D\), let \(R\) be the subgroup of \(L\) generated by the elements of the form \(E-E'-E''\) for each exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow0\]
of \(C\). We set \(K_D(C)=L/R\). The map \(\varphi\) is obtained by composing the two canonical maps \(\operatorname{Ob}D\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow L/R\).
When there is no danger of confusion, we may abbreviate the notation: \(K_D(C)=K(D)\).
2. The case of triangulated categories.
Let \(C\) be a triangulated category and \(G\) an abelian group. We say that a function \(f\) from \(C\) into \(G\) is additive if for every distinguished triangle
\[\xymatrix{&Z\ar[dl]_w\\X\ar[rr]_u&&Y\ar[ul]_v}\]
we have \(f(Y)=f(X)+f(Z)\).
Given an additive function \(f\), the following relations are immediate consequences of the definition:
a) \(f(0)=0\)
b) \(f(X[n])=(-1)^nf(X)\)
c) \(f(X)=f(Y)\) if \(X\) and \(Y\) are isomorphic.
d) \(f(X\oplus Y)=f(X)+f(Y)\)
Indeed, the following triangles are distinguished

\[\xymatrix@C=2.5em{&0\ar[dl]\\X\ar[rr]_{1_X}&&X\ar[ul]},\qquad \xymatrix@C=2.5em{&X[1]\ar[dl]\\X\ar[rr]&&0\ar[ul]},\qquad \xymatrix@C=2.5em{&0\ar[dl]\\X\ar[rr]^u&&Y\ar[ul]},\qquad \xymatrix@C=2.5em{&Y[1]\ar[dl]\\X\oplus Y\ar[rr]&&X\ar[ul]}\]

(\(u\) an isomorphism). To form the last triangle, we have used the fact that the sum of two distinguished triangles is distinguished. As in the case of abelian categories, we obtain in the obvious way a functor \(A:\mathbf{Ab}\longrightarrow\mathbf{Ab}\), which associates with each abelian group \(G\) the abelian group \(A(G)\) of additive functions on \(C\) with values in \(G\). This functor is representable and it is easy to construct a representing pair \((K(C),\operatorname{cl}_C)\). Indeed, we first consider the free abelian group \(\mathbb Z^{(\operatorname{Ob}C)}\) generated by the objects of \(C\) and then the subgroup \(R\) generated by the elements of the form \(X-Y-Z\), where
\[\xymatrix{&Z\ar[dl]\\Y\ar[rr]&&X\ar[ul]}\]
is a distinguished triangle. The quotient group \(\mathbb Z^{(\operatorname{Ob}C)}/R\) will be \(K(C)\) and \(\operatorname{cl}_C\) is obtained by composing the two canonical maps:
\[\operatorname{Ob}C\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^{(\operatorname{Ob}C)} \longrightarrow K(C).\]
3. Functorial character.
\(C\) and \(C'\) being two triangulated categories, let \(T:C\longrightarrow C'\) be an exact functor from \(C\) into \(C'\), that is to say an additive, "graded" functor which transforms

distinguished triangles into distinguished triangles. Let us denote as above by \(A,A':\mathbf{Ab}\longrightarrow\mathbf{Ab}\) the functors associated with the categories \(C\) and \(C'\). From this results a functorial morphism \(A'\longrightarrow A\) of "composition with \(T\)", hence a unique homomorphism \(K(T):K(C)\longrightarrow K(C')\) such that the diagram
\[\xymatrix{{\operatorname{Ob}C}\ar[r]^T\ar[d]_{\operatorname{cl}_C}& {\operatorname{Ob}C'}\ar[d]^{\operatorname{cl}_{C'}}\\ K(C)\ar[r]_{K(T)}&K(C')}\]

is commutative. It follows that if the functors \(T,T_1:C\longrightarrow C'\) are isomorphic, then \(K(T)=K(T_1)\). In particular, if the functor \(T\) is an equivalence of categories, then \(K(T)\) is an isomorphism.
If \(C''\) is a third triangulated category and \(F:C\times C'\longrightarrow C''\) a bifunctor exact in each variable, then it is clear that there results a bilinear map
\[K(F):K(C)\times K(C')\longrightarrow K(C''),\]
such that the following relation holds:
\[K(F)(\operatorname{cl}_C(X),\operatorname{cl}_{C'}(X')) =\operatorname{cl}_{C''}(F(X,X')).\]

blocks.title: Proposition 3.1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1.

Let \(A\) be a triangulated category, \(B\) a thick triangulated subcategory of \(A\), \(A/B\) the quotient category of \(A\) by \(B\), \(Q:A\longrightarrow A/B\) the canonical quotient functor, \(i:B\longrightarrow A\) the inclusion functor. Then the following sequence is exact:

\[K(B)\xrightarrow{K(i)}K(A)\xrightarrow{K(Q)}K(A/B)\longrightarrow0.\]
Proof. It is clear that there exists a natural homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Coker}(K(i))\xrightarrow{\alpha}K(A/B).\]
On the other hand there exists an obvious map
\[\operatorname{Ob}(A/B)\xrightarrow{\gamma}\operatorname{Coker}(K(i)),\]

since \(\operatorname{Ob}(A/B)=\operatorname{Ob}(A)\). Let \(X,Y\in\operatorname{Ob}(A/B)\) and suppose that they are isomorphic. We want to prove that \(\gamma(X)=\gamma(Y)\). The category \(A/B\) is obtained by a calculus of right or left fractions, the multiplicative system \(S\) being constituted by the set of morphisms \(f\) which are contained in a distinguished triangle \((X',Y',Z',f,g,h)\), where \(Z'\) is an object of \(B\) (\(S\) is a saturated multiplicative system). It then follows that there exist \(X_1\) and \(X_1\xrightarrow{s}X\), \(X_1\xrightarrow{t}Y\) such that \(s,t\in S\). It therefore suffices for us to prove that \(\gamma(X)=\gamma(X_1)\), which follows from the fact that there exists a distinguished triangle of the form \((X_1,X,Z,s,\ldots)\) with \(Z\in\operatorname{Ob}B\).

The map \(\gamma\) is an additive function. Indeed, \((X,Y,Z,f,g,h)\) being a distinguished triangle of \(A/B\), we must prove that \(\gamma(X)+\gamma(Z)=\gamma(Y)\). But the relation now follows, because the triangle \((X,Y,Z,f,g,h)\) is isomorphic to a distinguished triangle of \(A\). Consequently, we obtain
\[\beta:K(A/B)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Coker}(K(i)),\]
and it is immediate to verify that \(\beta\) is inverse to \(\alpha\).

pages.title: 4. Comparison with the case of abelian categories.

blocks.html: 4. Comparison with the case of abelian categories.

\(C\) being an abelian category, \(D(C)\) the derived category of \(C\), we denote as usual by \(D^b(C)\) the full subcategory of \(D(C)\) generated by the bounded complexes. If we associate with each object \(X\) of \(C\) the complex all of whose components are zero, except the one in dimension zero which is equal to \(X\), we obtain a homomorphism
\[K(C)\xrightarrow{\mu}K(D^b(C)).\]
This homomorphism is in fact an isomorphism. Indeed, we can use Proposition 3.1 to construct an inverse of \(\mu\), taking into account that
\[D^b(C)\simeq K^{b,b}(C)/K^{b,\varnothing}(C),\]
\(K^{b,\varnothing}(C)\) being the thick subcategory of \(K^{b,b}(C)\) of acyclic complexes. Let \(X^\bullet=(X_i)\in\operatorname{Ob}(K^{b,b}(C))\). If we set
\[f(X^\bullet)=\sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{cl}_C(X_i),\]

we obtain an additive function from \(K^{b,b}(C)\) with values in \(K(C)\). Indeed, we first observe that if \(X^\bullet\) and \(Y^\bullet\) are homotopic, then \(f(X^\bullet)=f(Y^\bullet)\), since \(f(X^\bullet)=f(H(X^\bullet))\). There thus results a homomorphism
\[K(D^b(C))\xrightarrow{\lambda}K(C)\]
and it is immediate to verify that it is the inverse of \(\mu\).

The invariant \(K(C)\) being often without interest when \(C\) is "too big", it is of interest to relativize it with respect to a subcategory \(C_0\) of \(C\). So let \(C_0\) be a full subcategory of \(C\). We can define on the one hand the group \(K_0(C)=K_{C_0}(C)\). Suppose on the other hand that the following condition is fulfilled:
(A) \(C_0\) is stable under finite direct sums.
It is then easy to define the triangulated category \(D_0^b(C)\) by using the complexes (with bounded cohomology) of \(C\) having as components objects of the subcategory \(C_0\), and passing to the
quotient by the quasi-isomorphisms of such complexes. As above, there results a homomorphism
\[(*)\qquad K_0(C)\longrightarrow K(D_0^b(C)).\]
Suppose that the subcategory \(C_0\) satisfies in addition the following condition (B):
(B) If \(X,Y\in\operatorname{Ob}C_0\) and \(X\xrightarrow{u}Y\) is an epimorphism (in \(C\) of course), then \(\operatorname{Ker}u\in\operatorname{Ob}C_0\).
Reasoning as above, one can show that the homomorphism (*) is an isomorphism.
The canonical inclusion functor \(D_0^b(C)\longrightarrow D^b(C)\) is not in general fully faithful. However:

blocks.title: Proposition 4.1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1.

Suppose that the subcategory \(C_0\) satisfies conditions (A), (B) as well as the following condition (C):

(C) Every diagram

\[\xymatrix{A\ar[r]^u&B&L\ar[l]^v}\]

where \(u\) is an epimorphism and \(L\in\operatorname{Ob}C_0\), can be completed to a commutative diagram:

\[\xymatrix{A\ar[r]^u&B\\L_1\ar[u]\ar[r]_w&L\ar[u]_v}\]

such that \(L_1\in\operatorname{Ob}C_0\) and \(w\) is an epimorphism.

Under these conditions, the canonical functors

\[D_0^b(C)\longrightarrow D^b(C)\] \[D_0^-(C)\longrightarrow D^-(C)\]

are fully faithful. Moreover, the essential image of \(D_0^b(C)\) (resp. \(D_0^-(C)\)) is stable under direct factors.
The proof of this proposition essentially uses a criterion of Verdier ([2], 4-2 Théorème) and will be given in the first exposé of the seminar on Riemann-Roch (SGA 6 I 2.7).

blocks.title: Corollary.
blocks.html:
Corollary.

Suppose moreover that the subcategory \(C_0\) is stable under Ker and Coker, hence an abelian category, and let us denote by \(D^b(C)_0\) the full triangulated subcategory of \(D^b(C)\) generated by the complexes of \(D^b(C)\) whose cohomology objects are isomorphic to objects of \(C_0\). Then the inclusion functor

\[D^b(C_0)\longrightarrow D^b(C)_0\]

is an equivalence between the two categories, and consequently we obtain the sequence of isomorphisms

\[K(C_0)\simeq K_0(C)\simeq K(D_0^b(C))\simeq K(D^b(C)_0).\]

(*)

Observation. Suppose that the subcategory \(C_0\) satisfies conditions (A) and (B) and that each object of \(C\) admits a finite left resolution by elements of \(C_0\). In this case, \(C_0\) also satisfies condition (C) and the canonical functor
\[D_0^b(C)\longrightarrow D^b(C)\]
is in fact an equivalence of categories. Consequently, we have the isomorphism \(K_{C_0}(C)\simeq K(C)\).
5. Pseudo-coherent complexes.

Let \(C\) be an abelian category and \(C_0\) a full subcategory of \(C\) satisfying conditions (A), (B), (C). We shall denote by \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) the essential image of \(D_0^-(C)\) under the canonical functor \(D_0^-(C)\longrightarrow D(C)\). The objects of \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) will be called \(C_0\)-pseudo-coherent complexes. Thus a complex of \(C\) is \(C_0\)-pseudo-coherent if it is isomorphic in \(D(C)\) to a complex which is bounded above and whose components are objects of \(C_0\).

The category \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(D(C)\), in the sense that every distinguished triangle of \(D(C)\) two of whose vertices are objects of the subcategory \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is isomorphic to a triangle whose three objects are objects of the subcategory \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\).

First of all, it is clear that the subcategory \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is stable under the translation automorphism \(T:D(C)\longrightarrow D(C)\). To prove, then, that \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(D(C)\), it suffices to verify that if \(U^\bullet\), \(V^\bullet\) are objects of \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) and if the triangle:
\[\xymatrix{&W^\bullet\ar[dl]_w\\U^\bullet\ar[rr]_u&&V^\bullet\ar[ul]_v}\]

is distinguished (for the triangulated category structure of \(D(C)\)), then \(W^\bullet\) is also an object of \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\). By hypothesis, there exist complexes \(X^\bullet\), \(Y^\bullet\), isomorphic in \(D(C)\) to \(U^\bullet\) resp. \(V^\bullet\), whose components are objects of \(C_0\). We obtain the square
which is commutative (in \(D(C)\)):
\[\xymatrix{U^\bullet\ar[r]^u\ar[d]_{\sim}&V^\bullet\ar[d]^{\sim}\\X^\bullet\ar[r]_f&Y^\bullet}.\]

But, after possibly replacing \(X^\bullet\) by an isomorphic one, we may assume, thanks to the fact that the inclusion functor \(D_0^-(C)\longrightarrow D^-(C)\) is fully faithful (prop. 4.1), that \(f\) is a genuine morphism of complexes. Let \(Z^\bullet\) be the "mapping cylinder" of \(f\) and \(g:Y^\bullet\longrightarrow Z^\bullet\) the canonical morphism. By the very construction, the components of \(Z^\bullet\) are objects of \(C_0\) (condition (A)). On the other hand
\[\xymatrix{&Z^\bullet\ar[dl]_h\\X^\bullet\ar[rr]_f&&Y^\bullet\ar[ul]_g}\]
being a distinguished triangle in \(D(C)\), it follows that \(Z^\bullet\) is isomorphic to \(W^\bullet\).

The category \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is thus endowed with a triangulated category structure: a triangle in \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) will be distinguished if and only if it is distinguished in the triangulated category \(D(C)\).
The intersection of \(D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) with \(D^b(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) will be denoted \(D_0^b(C)_{\mathrm{coh}}\).
An object \(M\) of \(C\) will be called pseudo-coherent if it is so as a complex reduced to degree zero ; one proves that it is necessary and sufficient
for this that it admit a left resolution whose components are objects of \(C_0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.

A complex \(X\) is \(C_0\)-pseudo-coherent if and only if it is isomorphic to a complex which is bounded on the right and whose components are \(C_0\)-pseudo-coherent.
The proof of this proposition will appear in the first exposé of the seminar on Riemann-Roch (SGA 6 I 2.7).
6. Perfect complexes.

An object of the category \(D(C)\) will be called a \(C_0\)-perfect complex if it is isomorphic to an object of \(D_0^b(C)\), that is to say to a bounded complex all of whose components are in \(C_0\). We shall denote by \(D_0(C)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) the full subcategory of \(D(C)\) generated by the perfect complexes.

The category \(D_0(C)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) is thus nothing other than the essential image of \(D_0^b(C)\) under the inclusion functor \(D_0^b(C)\longrightarrow D(C)\). It is easy to see that \(D_0(C)_{\mathrm{parf}}\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(D(C)\), and is consequently endowed with a triangulated category structure.
7. An important particular case.

Let \(A\) be a ring and \(C\) the category of \(A\)-modules (left ones, for example). Let us denote by \(C_0\) the full subcategory of \(C\) generated by the projective \(A\)-modules of finite type. The subcategory \(C_0\) satisfies conditions (A), (B),
(C). In this case we use the following notations:
\[ \begin{aligned} D^-(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}&=D_0^-(C)_{\mathrm{coh}},\\ D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}&=D_0^b(C)_{\mathrm{coh}},\\ D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}&=D_0(C)_{\mathrm{parf}}. \end{aligned} \]
The complexes of \(D(C)\) which are \(C_0\)-pseudo-coherent (resp. \(C_0\)-perfect) will be called simply pseudo-coherent complexes (resp. perfect complexes).
It will be interesting to consider the following groups:
\[ K_\bullet(A)=K\bigl(D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\bigr), \qquad K^\bullet(A)=K\bigl(D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\bigr). \]
As regards the group \(K^\bullet(A)\), we have (prop. 4.1, corollary):
\[ K^\bullet(A)=K\bigl(D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\bigr)\simeq K(D_0^b(C))\simeq K_{C_0}(C), \]
which shows that it is thus also the classical "group of classes of projective modules of finite type" of \(A\).

In the particular case of a noetherian ring \(A\), the group \(K_\bullet(A)\) also admits a classical description. For this, let \(C_1\) be the abelian category of modules of finite type. We then have
\[K_\bullet(A)\simeq K(C_1).\]
Indeed, using Proposition 4.1, we have
\[K(C_1)\simeq K(D^b(C_1))\simeq K(D_{C_1}^b(C))\]
for we immediately have \(D^b(C_1)\simeq D_{C_1}^b(C)\).
In fact, if \(A\) is noetherian and \(L^\bullet\in D^-(A)\), then it follows from Proposition 5.1 that \(L^\bullet\) is pseudo-coherent if and only if the \(H^i(L^\bullet)\) are of finite type.
For an arbitrary ring \(A\), we have a homomorphism
\[K^\bullet(A)\xrightarrow{\gamma_A}K_\bullet(A),\]
which comes from the inclusion functor
\[(*)\qquad D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\longrightarrow D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}.\]

In general, the homomorphism \(\gamma_A\) is neither injective nor surjective. In the particular case of a regular ring \(A\), every \(A\)-module \(M\) of finite type has finite projective dimension and it follows, thanks to 5.1, that the inclusion functor (*) is in fact an equivalence between the two categories. In this case, the homomorphism \(\gamma_A\) is thus an isomorphism.
8. Tor of complexes.

Let \(A\) be a ring (not necessarily commutative) and \({}_AC\) (resp. \(C_A\)) the abelian category of left (resp. right) \(A\)-modules. By \(K^-({}_AC)\) we shall denote the triangulated category of complexes of \({}_AC\) bounded above and by \({}_AD\) the full triangulated subcategory of \(K^-({}_AC)\) of complexes whose components are flat (left) \(A\)-modules.
Let \(X^\bullet\) be an object of \(K^-(C_A)\), and let us consider the functor
\[\Phi_{X^\bullet}=X^\bullet\otimes_A\mathord{:}K^-({}_AC) \longrightarrow K^-(\mathrm{Ab}).\]
Under these conditions, the following pro-
positions are true:
1) Every acyclic object of \({}_AD\) is transformed by \(\Phi_{X^\bullet}\) into an acyclic object of \(K^-(\mathrm{Ab})\).
Indeed, \(N^\bullet\) being an object of \({}_AD\) such that \(Z^i(N^\bullet)\), \(B^i(N^\bullet)\), \(H^i(N^\bullet)\) are flat, we have the classical isomorphism:
\[ \bigoplus_{p+q=n}H_p(X^\bullet)\otimes H_q(N^\bullet) \longrightarrow H_n(X^\bullet\otimes_A N^\bullet). \]
2) Every object \(Y^\bullet\) of \(K^-({}_AC)\) is the target of a quasi-isomorphism whose source is an object of \({}_AD\).
For this it suffices to take a Cartan-Eilenberg resolution. We can therefore apply Theorem 2 of §2, n°2 of [2] to conclude that \(\Phi_{X^\bullet}\) admits a left derived functor:
\[L\Phi_{X^\bullet}:D^-({}_AC)\longrightarrow D^-(\mathrm{Ab}).\]
The object \(L\Phi_{X^\bullet}(Y)\) can be obtained, up to isomorphism, in the following way:

Let \(P^\bullet\) be an object of \({}_AD\) and \(P^\bullet\longrightarrow Y^\bullet\) a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(L\Phi_{X^\bullet}(Y^\bullet)\) is isomorphic to \(Q(X^\bullet\otimes P^\bullet)\), where we have denoted as usual by \(Q\) the canonical functor \(K^-(\mathrm{Ab})\longrightarrow D^-(\mathrm{Ab})\). It therefore follows that for each object \(Y^\bullet\) of \(D^-({}_AC)\) the functor
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} K^-(C_A)&\longrightarrow&D^-(\mathrm{Ab})\\ X^\bullet&\longmapsto&L\Phi_{X^\bullet}(Y^\bullet) \end{array} \]
is exact and vanishes on acyclic complexes, whence an exact bifunc-
tor
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} D^-(C_A)\times D^-({}_AC)&\longrightarrow&D^-(\mathrm{Ab})\\ (X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)&\longmapsto&X^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A Y^\bullet. \end{array} \]
Passing to homology, we define the \(\operatorname{Tor}_i^A(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\) (cf also [1], 6.3). In the particular case of a commutative ring \(A\) we obtain in fact an exact bi-functor
\[D^-(C)\times D^-(C)\longrightarrow D^-(C),\]
the tensor product in the sense of derived categories \((C=C_A={}_AC)\).

blocks.title: Definition.
blocks.html:
Definition.

We shall say that the complex \(X\) of \(D(C_A)\) is of finite tor-dimension if it is in \(D^-\) and if there exists \(n_0\in\mathbb Z^+\) such that \(\operatorname{Tor}_n^A(X,Y)=0\) for every \(Y\in\operatorname{Ob}({}_AC)\) and \(n>n_0\).
We shall use the notion of tor-dimension to obtain a characterization of perfect complexes:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.1.

Let \(A\) be a ring (not necessarily commutative) and \(L^\bullet\) a complex of \(A\)-modules. The following conditions are equivalent:

a) \(L^\bullet\) is perfect.

b) \(L^\bullet\) is pseudo-coherent, with bounded cohomology, and of finite tor-dimension.(*)
To carry out the proof, we use the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma.
blocks.html:
Lemma. (Bourbaki, Alg. Comm. I, §2, ex. 15).

Every \(A\)-module \(E\) which is flat and of finite presentation is projective.
Proof of the lemma. Let \(u:F\longrightarrow F'\) be an epimorphism. We must prove that \(\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,F')\) is an epimorphism.
It suffices for us to prove that for every divisible group \(G\),
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z}(\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,F'),G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z}(\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,F),G)\]
is a monomorphism. Now the diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z}(\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,F'),G)\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\sim}& \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z}(\operatorname{Hom}_A(E,F),G)\ar[d]^{\sim}\\ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z}(F',G)\otimes_A E\ar[r]& \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z}(F,G)\otimes_A E } \]
is commutative, and the vertical arrows are isomorphisms, thanks to the hypothesis made on \(E\).
Proof of Proposition 5.2. The implication a) \(\Rightarrow\) b) is immediate. To prove b) \(\Rightarrow\) a), let
\[\cdots\longrightarrow P_n\xrightarrow{d_n}\cdots\longrightarrow P_i\longrightarrow0\longrightarrow\cdots\]

be a complex isomorphic to \(L^\bullet\) in \(D(C)\), the \(P_j\) being projective \(A\)-modules of finite type. The complex \(L^\bullet\) being of finite tor-dimension and its cohomology being bounded, it follows that there exists \(n_0\in\mathbb Z\) such that:
I) The sequence
\[\cdots\longrightarrow P_{n_0-p}\xrightarrow{d_{n_0-p}}\cdots\longrightarrow P_{n_0}\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ker}d_{n_0+1}\longrightarrow0\]
is exact.
II) \(\operatorname{Ker}d_{n_0+1}\) is flat.
III) The complex
\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ker}d_{n_0+1}\longrightarrow P_{n_0+1}\longrightarrow\cdots \longrightarrow P_i\longrightarrow0\longrightarrow\]
is isomorphic to \(L^\bullet\) in \(D(C)\).
Now \(\operatorname{Ker}d_{n_0+1}\) is of finite presentation, hence projective of finite type thanks to the lemma, which proves 8.1.

If \(A\) is a commutative ring, and if \(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet\) are two perfect complexes, then their tensor product \(X^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A Y^\bullet\) (in the derived category) is again a perfect complex. We obtain a bifunctor
\[D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\times D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\longrightarrow D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\]

exact in each variable. Consequently, we define a product on \(K^\bullet(A)\), and it is easy to see that \(K^\bullet(A)\) is in this way endowed with the structure of a commutative ring, having a unit element. In fact, \(K^\bullet(A)\) can be endowed with the structure of a \(\lambda\)-ring (see for the details the Seminar on Riemann-Roch following the present one(*)).

The tensor product \(X^\bullet\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_A Y^\bullet\) of a perfect complex \(X^\bullet\) and a bounded pseudo-coherent complex \(Y^\bullet\) is a bounded pseudo-coherent complex (\(A\) still a commutative ring). Consequently, we can introduce on \(K_\bullet(A)\) a structure of \(K^\bullet(A)\)-module.
9. Functorial properties.

a) Let \(A\) and \(B\) be two rings (not necessarily commutative) and \(u:A\longrightarrow B\) a homomorphism. Every left (resp. right) \(B\)-module \(M\) can be endowed with a structure of left (resp. right) \(A\)-module which will be denoted by \(M_{[u]}\). In particular \(B_{[u]}\) is a right \(A\)-module.
The functor
\[E\longmapsto B_{[u]}\otimes_A E\]
allows us to define the exact functor
\[D(A)_{\mathrm{parf}}\longrightarrow D(B)_{\mathrm{parf}}\]
and consequently the homomorphism:
\[K^\bullet(A)\xrightarrow{u^*}K^\bullet(B).\]
We obtain in fact a covariant functor from the category of rings (resp. of commutative rings) into the category of abelian groups (resp. of commutative rings).
b) Suppose now that the left \(A\)-module \(B_{[u]}\) is pseudo-coherent. It then follows that the functor
\[F\longmapsto F_{[u]}\]
transforms a free \(B\)-module of finite type into a pseudo-coherent \(A\)-module. Using Proposition 5.1, we obtain an exact functor:
\[D^b(B)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^b(A)_{\mathrm{coh}}\]
and consequently a canonical homomorphism
\[K_\bullet(B)\xrightarrow{u_*}K_\bullet(A).\]
The homomorphisms \(u_*,u^*\) satisfy the following identity ("the projection formula"):
\[u_*\bigl(u^*(\alpha)\cdot\beta\bigr)=\alpha\bigl(u_*(\beta)\bigr) \quad\text{for }\alpha\in K^\bullet(A),\ \beta\in K_\bullet(B).\]
This easy formula will be proved in a more general form in the exposés of the seminar on Riemann-Roch (SGA 6 IV 2.11.1.2).
10. Relative construction.(*)
Let \(u:A\longrightarrow B\) be a ring homomorphism, and
\[D(B)\longrightarrow D(A)\]

the associated exact functor. We can consider in \(D(B)\) the complexes which are pseudo-coherent relative to \(A\), those of finite tor-dimension relative to \(A\), and also the complexes which are perfect relative to \(A\). In particular, it is appropriate to consider the triangulated subcategories
\[D(B)_{A\text{-}\mathrm{parf}},\quad D^b(B)_{A\text{-}\mathrm{coh}}\]
of \(D(B)\), generated by the complexes which are perfect (resp. pseudo-coherent and bounded relative to \(A\). These two triangulated categories allow us to define the following groups:
\[K^\bullet(B\mathrel{\mathrm{rel}}A)=K(D(B)_{A\text{-}\mathrm{parf}})\] \[K_\bullet(B\mathrel{\mathrm{rel}}A)=K(D^b(B)_{A\text{-}\mathrm{coh}}).\]
Examples. Let \(G\) be a group and \(\Lambda\) a commutative ring. The homomorphism \(\Lambda\xrightarrow{\varepsilon}\Lambda[G]\) thus allows us to define the groups
\[K^\bullet(\Lambda[G]\mathrel{\mathrm{rel}}\Lambda),\quad K_\bullet(\Lambda[G]\mathrel{\mathrm{rel}}\Lambda),\]
which will be denoted by \(R^\bullet_\Lambda(G)\), \(R^\Lambda_\bullet(G)\). In fact, \(R^\bullet_\Lambda(G)\) is a commutative ring and \(R^\Lambda_\bullet(G)\) a module over the latter.
Remarks.

a) If the ring \(\Lambda\) is not necessarily commutative, we can set by definition \(\Lambda[G]=\Lambda\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb ZG\) and consequently we obtain a homomorphism \(\Lambda\xrightarrow{\varepsilon}\Lambda[G]\). In this way, we can give a meaning to the groups \(R^\bullet_\Lambda(G)\), \(R^\Lambda_\bullet(G)\) even in the case of a non-commutative ring \(\Lambda\).

b) The preceding construction can be generalized to the case of a group prescheme (or a group pro-prescheme) \(G\) over \(\operatorname{Spec}\Lambda\), starting from the abelian category of \(\Lambda\)-modules on which \(G\) operates — which can no longer be made explicit in terms of modules over a ring — and the natural inclusion functor into the category of \(\Lambda\)-modules. (If \(\Lambda\) is a field, it can be made explicit in terms of modules over a pro-ring, and in the general case, in terms of comodules, at least if \(G\) is affine over \(\Lambda\), cf SGA3 I 4.7.2).
Bibliography.

[2] J. L. Verdier, Catégories Dérivées, I.H.E.S., 1963.(*)
